concepts.title: Continental Drift Theory
concepts.description: The theory that the continents were once joined together in a single supercontinent called Pangaea, which began to break apart about 200 million years ago.

concepts.title: Formation of Mountains
concepts.description: Mountains are formed through the process of orogenesis, which involves the movement of tectonic plates, resulting in the collision of continents and the formation of fold mountains.

concepts.title: Types of Plate Boundaries
concepts.description: There are three types of plate boundaries: Divergent (moving apart), Convergent (moving together), and Transform (sliding past each other).

concepts.title: Volcanic Landforms
concepts.description: Mnemonics: Volcanoes form mountains (V-FM), Shield volcanoes are rounded (S-R), and Cinder Cones are steep-sided (C-CS).

concepts.title: Understanding Weathering and Erosion
concepts.description: Students often confuse weathering and erosion, but weathering is the breakdown of rocks into smaller particles, while erosion is the removal and transportation of those particles away from the source.

concepts.title: Units of Measurement for Landforms
concepts.description: Students should remember that 1 km = 1000 meters, 1 meter = 100 centimeters, and 1 kilometer is equal to 1000 meters (or 1,000,000 millimeters).

concepts.title: Sea-Floor Spreading
concepts.description: Sea-floor spreading is the process by which new oceanic crust is created at mid-ocean ridges, where magma rises from the Earth's mantle to fill the gap between moving tectonic plates.

concepts.title: River Erosion
concepts.description: River erosion is the process by which rivers wear away the landscape through the action of water, resulting in the formation of valleys and canyons.
